title: Grout and tile cleaning paste
subtitle: A bicarbonate paste for discoloured bathroom and kitchen grout
excerpt: A thick paste of bicarbonate of soda and liquid castile soap that cleans discoloured grout and tile surfaces. Apply with an old toothbrush, scrub, and rinse. Works on limescale and general soiling; not a mould treatment.
sourceNotes: Bicarbonate of soda as a mild abrasive for tile and grout: standard household cleaning reference. Liquid castile soap as a surfactant. White wine vinegar as a rinse agent to dissolve any bicarbonate residue: the acid-base neutralisation prevents the chalky residue that bicarbonate leaves on surfaces if not fully rinsed.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 small batch (~100 g, sufficient for one tiled surface or shower unit)
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make fresh for each use; the paste dries out quickly.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Make fresh and use within 3 days. The paste dries out once made; add a few drops of water to restore if stored.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hard white mineral deposit of calcium carbonate that forms where hard water evaporates, particularly around taps, showers, and on tile grout. Bicarbonate of soda's mild abrasive action helps dislodge it; citric acid dissolves it more effectively for heavy deposits.

content.text: Grout is porous and picks up soap scum, mineral deposits, and general bathroom soiling. This paste uses bicarbonate of soda as a mild abrasive and liquid castile soap to lift the loosened soil with scrubbing. For

deposits specifically, a citric acid rinse after the paste is more effective at dissolving the mineral. This recipe is a general grout paste; it handles everyday soiling and moderate discolouration. Black mould in grout requires a different treatment and is outside the scope of this recipe.

items.prepNote: Add as a final rinse agent; dissolves bicarbonate residue and any light limescale. Do not mix with the paste directly; use as a separate rinse.

content.text: Combine the bicarbonate of soda and liquid castile soap in a small bowl and stir to form a thick paste. Apply directly onto the grout lines using an old toothbrush. Work the paste into the grout in short scrubbing strokes. Allow to sit for 5 to 10 minutes to soften the soiling.

Scrub along the grout lines with the toothbrush. Rinse with warm water. For a final rinse, wipe the surface with a cloth dampened in diluted white wine vinegar (equal parts vinegar and water); this neutralises any remaining bicarbonate and dissolves light limescale deposits. Do not apply the vinegar rinse directly to natural stone surfaces such as marble or limestone, which are damaged by acid.

Safe for ceramic tile, porcelain tile, and standard cement grout. Do not use on natural stone (marble, granite, travertine, limestone): the bicarbonate abrasive can scratch polished surfaces, and the vinegar rinse is acidic and can etch stone. For natural stone grout, use a stone-specific cleaner.
Make fresh for each use. The paste dries out quickly once made. Any unused portion can be stored in a lidded container for 3 days; add a few drops of water and stir to restore consistency before use.
